# Data overview

The imported envOcc dataset consists of `r length(bio_all_names)` data sources (Table \@ref(tab:datasources-tbl)). See the envOcc report for full detail about each dataset.

```{r datasources-tbl}

knitr::kable(bio_all_info |> 
               dplyr::select(Source = data_name_use,
                             Description = desc,
                             Extracted = modification_time,
                             Reference = ref),
             caption = "Sources of data aggregated for cleaning in this report") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling("striped")

```


## Data scale

The scale of the data is made up of the 'extent' of the data in space and time, and the 'grain', or size, of bins that records are placed in.

A settings YAML script file is used to set the extent and grain of the clean, with outputs stored in a directory structure set by the `name_env_out` function from `envFunc` `r cite_package("envFunc")`. This naming convention and directory structure is used to refer to a particular dataset by other projects.

### Data extent

The data extent is the geographic and temporal range of the data imported from envOcc, as well as the limit of records returned at the end of data cleaning. The geographic extent is generally set by the polygon 'aus_imcra_prov_dissolve' (Figure \@ref(fig:extentmap), blue). This polygon is the combination of the land area of all of Australia (excluding offshore territories) from the Interim Biogeographic Regionalisation for Australia (IBRA; https://www.dcceew.gov.au/environment/land/nrs/science/ibra) and the marine equivalent, the Integrated Marine and Coastal Regionalisation of Australia (IMCRA; https://fed.dcceew.gov.au/datasets/erin::integrated-marine-and-coastal-regionalisation-of-australia-imcra-v4-0-provincial-bioregions/about).

The temporal extent is a period of time described using the [ISO 8601](https://www.iso.org/iso-8601-date-and-time-format.html) standard. The time period covered is '`r settings$context$extent$ext_time`', i.e. `r readr::parse_number(settings$context$extent$ext_time)` `r envTargets::extract_temporal_grain(settings$context$extent)`s up to and including the last full calendar year.


```{r extentmap, fig.cap="Geographic extent of the data"}

m <- extent_map +
  tm_add_legend(type = "polygons", 
                col = "darkorange", fill = "white", lwd = 2, 
                title = paste0("Extent: ", envFunc::name_env_out(list(extent = settings$context$extent[1:4]))$extent)
  ) 
  
if(any(!is.null(c(settings$context$extent$filt_col, settings$context$extent$filt_col)),
       settings$context$extent$buffer > 0)) {
  
  extent_poly <- sfarrow::st_read_parquet(extent_sf_file) |>
    envFunc::make_aoi(filt_col = settings$context$extent$filt_col,
                      filt_level = settings$context$extent$filt_level)
  
  m <- m + 
    tm_shape(extent_poly) +
    tm_borders(col = "blue", lwd = 1)+
    tm_add_legend(type = "polygons", 
                  col = "blue", fill = NA, lwd = 1, 
                  title = paste0("Base polygon: ", settings$context$extent$vector)
    )
}
  
m <- m +
  tm_shape(region_taxa_sf) +
  tm_polygons(col = "blue", fill = NULL,
              lwd = 1) +
  tm_add_legend(type = "polygons", 
                col = "blue", fill = "white",
                title = paste0("Region taxa: ", settings$context$extent$region_taxa)
  ) +
  tm_layout(legend.bg.color = "white")


if(use_tmap_mode == "view"){
  m <- tmap_leaflet(m)
} 

m

```


### Region taxa {#region-taxa-context}

As well as the outer geographic boundary of records set by the extent, a limit on the taxa returned in `bio_clean` is set by a 'region taxa' filter. This removes any records of taxa that are found in the data, but do not occur in the specified region. For example, if the data extent is all of Australia, but 'region taxa' was set to South Australia, records would be returned from across Australia, but only for those taxa that occur in South Australia. It is specified by the name of a polygon in the envCleaned settings file, and forms part of the dataset name and directory structure produced by `name_env_out`. The region taxa polygon may be the same as the extent polygon.

Here, region taxa is set by the polygon `r settings$context$extent$region_taxa`  (Figure \@ref(fig:extentmap), blue).

### Data grain

The grain defines the size of bins used throughout the cleaning process. There is typically a cell size (resolution) defining a spatial bin, a period of time defining a temporal bin (e.g. year), and a taxonomic bin defining the lowest taxonomic group required (e.g. species).

Here, the spatial grain is `r settings$context$grain$res_x` x `r settings$context$grain$res_y`-metre grid cells, the temporal grain is '`r settings$context$grain$res_time`' (i.e. `r paste(gsub("[^0-9.-]", "", settings$context$grain$res_time), envTargets::extract_temporal_grain(settings$context$grain))`), and the taxonomic grain is `r settings$context$grain$taxonomic`.

This information is also stored in the directory structure using `name_env_out`: `r envFunc::name_env_out(list(grain=settings$context$grain))$grain`.
